parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Noadiah Russell of Middletown — Yale founding trustee 1701
Rev. Noadiah Russell of Middletown — Yale founding trustee 1701

cross_connections.display_label: Married the sister of his own sister's husband, both Pierpont-Russell siblings.

bio.bio_blurb: North Haven farmer with twelve children

military_service.notes: FindAGrave: Lieut Joseph Pierpont; North Haven farmer.

narrative_blocks.header: Double sibling marriage joined Pierponts and Russells
narrative_blocks.body: He married Hannah Russell around 1725 while his sister Mary married Hannah's brother William the same year. Two siblings on each side, married into a single family in one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His marriage joined two Yale-founding families
narrative_blocks.body: His father Rev. James Pierpont was a Yale founding trustee in 1701. His wife's father Rev. Noadiah Russell was another.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children at his North Haven farm
narrative_blocks.body: He farmed land about ten miles northeast of New Haven through the 1730s and 1740s. Several of his twelve children died in infancy, including the first James in 1727.

bio.bio_blurb: Brick maker, Episcopal founder, warden fifty years

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Royalist Episcopal church at North Haven
narrative_blocks.body: He was a founder and the first senior warden of St. John's Church of England in North Haven, elected at age thirty-two in 1761. The rector and majority of members were Royalists during the Revolution but accepted the new government afterward.

narrative_blocks.header: Senior warden for fifty years until death
narrative_blocks.body: He continued as senior warden of St. John's, with few exceptions, for fifty years — until his death in 1820 at age ninety-one. He outlived his wife Elizabeth Frost by sixteen years.

narrative_blocks.header: Brick maker who served the Revolution
narrative_blocks.body: His occupation was brick maker in North Haven. Despite his Episcopal church's Royalist majority, he served in the Revolution — a fact recorded on his gravestone in the Old Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Louisa died thirty days after marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Theodosia's daughter Louisa Walter married Timothy Bishop on April 3, 1800 — and died on May 3, 1800, just thirty days after the marriage. Louisa was twenty. The compressed marriage-and-death cycle within a single calendar month captured the period's vulnerability to acute disease across all socioeconomic groups, even within the Pierpont-Walter household at New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-granddaughter named Theodocia Pierpont in her honor
narrative_blocks.body: A generation after Theodosia's 1806 death, her son William Walter's daughter named her own daughter Theodocia Pierpont Walter Hayden (born August 20, 1817) — honoring the Pierpont great-grandmother through both first name and middle name. The naming carried the Theodosia-and-Pierpont identity forward across three generations of women in the family.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson William Walter Hayden drowned in childhood
narrative_blocks.body: Theodosia's great-grandson William Walter Hayden — son of Theodocia Pierpont Walter Hayden — drowned on August 2, 1850 in Windsor Connecticut at age twelve. The cascading family losses — daughter at twenty in 1800, great-granddaughter at twenty in 1838, great-great-grandson at twelve in 1850 — touched four consecutive generations of the Walter-Hayden line.

narrative_blocks.header: Born North Haven 1758, died New Haven 1806
narrative_blocks.body: Theodosia Pierpont was born February 22, 1758 in North Haven Connecticut and died July 15, 1806 in New Haven Connecticut at age forty-eight. She married William Jay Walter on November 17, 1777 in North East Village, New Haven — he survived her by eighteen years, dying April 19, 1824. Her five documented children were Joel, Louisa, Lovina, Jerry, and William Jr.

cross_connections.display_label: Ebenezer Pierpont's wife Lydia Blakeslee (b.1768, d.1863) lived to age.

narrative_blocks.header: His five-thousand-dollar bequest transformed North Haven church
narrative_blocks.body: On Ebenezer Pierpont's death in 1854, North Haven Congregational Church 'came into a legacy of five thousand dollars on the death of one of its oldest members.' The bequest was 'a munificent gift and marked the turning point in the history of the church.' His widow Lydia Blakeslee Pierpont added another thousand dollars at her own 1863 death nine years later — six thousand total, a remarkable sum for a mid-century Connecticut parish congregation.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Lydia Blakeslee outlived him by nine years
narrative_blocks.body: Ebenezer married Lydia Blakeslee (born July 2, 1768) — daughter of Joel and Elizabeth Sackett Blakeslee. Lydia outlived him by nine years, dying July 22, 1863 in North Haven at age ninety-five, her own thousand-dollar bequest to the church supplementing his earlier five-thousand. The couple's combined legacy effectively endowed the North Haven Congregational Church mid-century operations.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and died North Haven, age eighty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Ebenezer Pierpont was born in 1771 in North Haven Connecticut and died March 4, 1854 in North Haven at approximately age eighty-three. His entire life unfolded within his birthplace town — a stability characteristic of the eighteenth-and-early-nineteenth-century New Haven County Pierpont families. His wife Lydia would live to ninety-five, an extraordinarily long life for her generation.

cross_connections.display_label: His Pierpont nephew became U.S. Attorney General under Grant

bio.bio_blurb: Yale 1751; fifty-nine years North Haven parish clerk

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1751 — fifty-nine years parish clerk
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1751 and served as parish clerk for fifty-nine years — first at the Congregational church, then at St. John's Episcopal in North Haven. The Congregational church refused to accept his resignation, so for four years he served as clerk of both simultaneously.

narrative_blocks.header: First Episcopalian appointed Justice of the Peace
narrative_blocks.body: He was the first Episcopalian appointed Justice of the Peace by the Connecticut Legislature. He served nine times in the Connecticut General Assembly, beginning in 1787 when North Haven was first incorporated as a town.

narrative_blocks.header: Lightning struck six on a sabbath-day-house bench
narrative_blocks.body: On July 29, 1771, Capt. Pierpont, his wife Lydia, and four others were struck by lightning while seated on one bench in a sabbath-day-house. Three horses and a colt were killed instantly; all six humans survived.

narrative_blocks.header: Captain in the Connecticut Militia, served the Revolution
narrative_blocks.body: He was commissioned first lieutenant of the Ninth Company, Second Regiment Connecticut Militia in 1760, and captain in 1764. His gravestone confirms he served in the Revolution.

narrative_blocks.header: The first Pierpont to put down Waterbury roots
narrative_blocks.body: A Revolutionary War soldier, Ezra Pierpont was the first of his family to settle the East Farms district of Waterbury, arriving in 1801 after years of buying and expanding farmland in nearby Prospect. His descendants held the land for a century and founded a family association in 1923 whose reunions ran for ninety years.

narrative_blocks.header: Third in his generation to marry a Blakeslee
narrative_blocks.body: Ezra married Mary Blakeslee in 1783. He was the third Pierpont of his generation to wed into the Blakeslee family, after his kinswomen Elizabeth and Mehitable Pierpont had each married a Blakeslee man.

narrative_blocks.header: Forty-four years as North Haven Town Clerk
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Pierpont succeeded his father as Town Clerk of North Haven Connecticut and served in the role for forty-four years — from about 1807 until his death in 1851. The continuous-tenure record was one of the longer documented town-clerkships in early-Republic Connecticut, and combined with his cabinetmaking work, it positioned him as 'one of North Haven's leading citizens.'

narrative_blocks.header: Five legislative terms, Constitutional Convention delegate
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Pierpont represented North Haven in the Connecticut General Assembly for five terms (1813, 1815, 1818, 1824, and one other), served as a Justice of the Peace for many years, and was a delegate to the Connecticut Constitutional Convention in Hartford. The combined civic portfolio — Town Clerk, JP, state representative, constitutional delegate — built one of the more complete antebellum local-leadership Connecticut careers.

narrative_blocks.header: Pierpont Homestead 1809, now local historic district
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1809 Daniel Pierpont purchased the Pierpont Homestead at 29 State Street North Haven — a Georgian Colonial built 1785 by Stephen Jacobs to which he added a rear addition in 1846. The homestead and the adjacent store run by son Rufus for over a hundred years became 'the center of life at the Pines Bridge in North Haven' and is today a local historic district. The house does not face State Street because the road originally curved to a ford across the river.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and died North Haven, age seventy-six
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Pierpont was born May 16, 1775 in North Haven Connecticut and died there November 16, 1851 at age seventy-six, buried at Old Cemetery North Haven. He had married Esther Humiston on September 26, 1799 — she outlived him by thirteen years, dying August 17, 1864 in New Haven at age ninety. Their seven children were Bede, Elias, Esther/Stiles, Harriet, Sally, Jared, and Rufus.

death.notes: Died in infancy per EH; ~2yr estimated, born between siblings James (1732) and Benjamin (1735)

narrative_blocks.header: Dorothy died young
narrative_blocks.body: Dorothy died young; the dates were not recorded.

narrative_blocks.header: Fourth in his generation to marry a Blakeslee
narrative_blocks.body: His marriage to Sarah Blakeslee in October 1765 was the fourth Blakeslee match in his Pierpont generation — Elizabeth, Mehitable, and Ezra Pierpont had all married Blakeslees first. The North Haven Pierpont and Blakeslee families intertwined deeply across three generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Old/New Style calendar muddied his birth year
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker's 1909 record gives his birth as January 7, 1735, while online sources give 1734 — the difference reflects the Old Style/New Style calendar shift. Britain and the colonies adopted the Gregorian calendar in 1752, retroactively shifting January-March dates from the prior year.

naming_inspiration.name_element: Pierpont (given to son Jared Pierpont Brockett)
naming_inspiration.description: Son Jared Pierpont Brockett (b. Mar 18, 1810) was given the Pierpont name as a middle name to honor Timothy's mother's family (the Pierpont line). The maternal-surname-as-middle-name was a deliberate naming convention preserving the Pierpont ancestry forward across this branch.

narrative_blocks.header: Built the first tannery in Saratoga, New York
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Brockett built the first tannery in Saratoga New York — when the place was a frontier settlement of log huts. The tannery was the founding industrial establishment of what became one of New York's noted nineteenth-century resort and industrial towns. He had reached Saratoga by sailing up the Hudson River to Albany, then crossing to Schenectady (then a small town), then taking the bridle path north to Saratoga.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children including farmer and merchant sons
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy and Olive Root had eight children: Lucy (b.1794), Polly (b.1796 d.1853 never married), Abel (1799-1803 died young), Laura (b.1803 d.1803 died young), Hannah (b.1804 married Justus Eli Allen of Oxford NY), Thomas (b.1806 farmer in Black River County NY), Jared Pierpont Brockett (b. March 18, 1810), and Ira Brockett (b.1812 married Mary Mairs, successful merchant). Two of the eight died as infants.

narrative_blocks.header: Named son Jared Pierpont for mother's family
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy gave his fifth surviving son the middle name Pierpont — Jared Pierpont Brockett — honoring his own mother's Pierpont family ancestry. The maternal-surname-as-middle-name was a deliberate genealogical naming convention common to early-nineteenth-century New England families seeking to preserve memory of mother's surname forward across generations of children who would otherwise carry only the father's line.

units: 2nd Regiment, 7th Company under Captain Ambrose Tuttle (Connecticut militia)
military_service.role: Connecticut militia enlistee under Captain Ambrose Tuttle's company.

narrative_blocks.header: Dreamed his vessel sank that night
narrative_blocks.body: Hezekiah Brockett was a sea captain engaged in the New Haven-West Indies trade. On one voyage when he did not accompany his vessel, he had a dream that the vessel had gone down — the impression so vivid that he awoke his wife and told her his dream; the vessel was never heard from, and he always believed it had gone down that night. He never went to sea again after the premonition was confirmed.

narrative_blocks.header: Sea captain became Mount Carmel cooperage owner
narrative_blocks.body: After abandoning sea travel following his premonition dream, Hezekiah engaged in the cooperage business at Mount Carmel Connecticut — barrel-making for the New Haven-West Indies trade he had previously sailed in. Cooperage at the time was 'one of the leading industries of the country and an important factor in the trade between New Haven and the West Indies.' The career pivot from sea to shore kept him in the same trade network without exposing him to maritime risk.

narrative_blocks.header: War of 1812 muster roll, Captain Tuttle's company
narrative_blocks.body: Hezekiah Brockett's name appears on the War of 1812 muster roll, Second Regiment, Seventh Company, under Captain Ambrose Tuttle of the Connecticut militia. His brothers Pierpont and Jesse Brockett had both also served — Pierpont as ensign-then-lieutenant under Commander Abraham Hubbard at Groton and New London, and Jesse as Lieutenant 1812 / Captain 1815 in the Connecticut militia.

narrative_blocks.header: Six children with Mehitabel Candee, Hamden CT
narrative_blocks.body: Hezekiah Brockett married Mehitabel Candee about 1791 (born ca.1775, daughter of Zaccheus Candee and Rebecca Smith). They resided in Hamden Connecticut and had six children: Ephraim, Justus T. (later moved Chenango County NY), Hezekiah Jr., Cynthia (married Arba Dickerman), Charles (manufacturer, married Amelia Bristol), and Julia (born and died October-November 1800 age 21 days). Mehitabel died November 21, 1840 Hamden; Hezekiah died September 22, 1849 age eighty, buried Old Graveyard, Mount Carmel, Hamden.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons died violent maritime deaths
narrative_blocks.body: Three of Lyman Brockett's sons died violent maritime deaths within the same generation: Miles (born 1805) was killed by a shark while in the West Indies around 1826 at age twenty-one; Loyal (born 1811) died at sea in 1829 of yellow fever as a ship officer at age eighteen; and Lyman Goodsell (born 1823) was a ship officer who died in the East Indies. The compressed family loss across three sons in the merchant and whaling trades captured the maritime risk of mid-nineteenth-century American shipping.

narrative_blocks.header: Four or five sons in shipping and whaling
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond the three sons who died at sea, Lyman's son Lewis (born 1808) died in New Haven after returning from a three-year whaling voyage, unmarried — suggesting illness contracted on the voyage. The family was a maritime-trade family in concentration: five of nine children had ocean-going careers. The eldest son Merrick later moved inland to Peoria Illinois, where his father Lyman eventually joined him.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut to Illinois migration in old age
narrative_blocks.body: After his New Haven-area maritime family had been scattered or lost to the sea, Lyman Brockett moved to Rosefield Illinois in his old age and died there around 1857. He is buried at Peoria Illinois — joining his eldest son Merrick who had migrated west earlier. His wife Hannah Goodsell of Watertown Connecticut had predeceased him by about two years in 1855.

narrative_blocks.header: Born North Haven 1780, nine children with Hannah
narrative_blocks.body: Lyman Brockett was born July 25, 1780 in North Haven Connecticut (baptized September 7, 1780) and married Hannah Goodsell of Watertown Connecticut. Nine children: Merrick, Miles, Lewis, Loyal, Lucy Charlotte (married Isaac Jones and removed to Oregon; remarried William Jones), Russell (died in infancy), Harvey Russell (named for grandmother Hannah Russell Pierpont's family), Mary Ann, and Lyman Goodsell.

notable.notable_blurb: Perished in the Great Chicago Fire of 1871

narrative_blocks.header: She perished in the Great Chicago Fire
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy Barnes Brockett died in the Great Chicago Fire of October 1871, the disaster that killed some three hundred people and leveled miles of the city. All four of her children had died before her.

narrative_blocks.header: All four children died before her
narrative_blocks.body: She outlived every one of her four children — and then died herself in the Great Chicago Fire of October 1871, among the roughly three hundred killed as the flames took three square miles of the city.

cross_connections.display_label: Aunt was the 'young lady in New Haven' Edwards described.
Great-granddaughter of the Yale founder Pierpont.
Brother-in-law twice over: married her sister Hannah.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Brockett brothers married two Pierpont sisters
narrative_blocks.body: She married Richard Brockett in March 1756; the year before, her sister Hannah had married Richard's brother Abel. Their children were double first cousins on both the Brockett and Pierpont sides.

narrative_blocks.header: Niece of Edwards's 'young lady in New Haven'
narrative_blocks.body: Her aunt Sarah Pierpont Edwards was the 'young lady in New Haven' Jonathan Edwards famously described in 1722, the year before he proposed marriage to her. Mary was Edwards's niece-by-marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-granddaughter of Yale's founder
narrative_blocks.body: Her great-grandfather Rev. James Pierpont was co-founder of Yale College in 1701 and pastor of First Church New Haven for nearly thirty years. The Brockett genealogy notes she was 'a grand-daughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker' through this same Pierpont line.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-four in North Haven
narrative_blocks.body: She died at North Haven, Connecticut on June 21, 1773 at age 34. She was buried at Montowese Cemetery; her husband Richard remarried Jemima Jacobs in 1790 and lived to age 85.

units: New Haven Militia and Alarm List under Col. Russell
military_service.source_citation: Sheldon B. Thorpe, 'North Haven Annals' — Col. Russell's 1779 order to officers of the Alarm List and Militia of New Haven endorsed names of Richard Brockett, Ebenezer Brockett, and Isaiah Brockett
military_service.role: Officer on the New Haven Alarm List — the call-up roster for civilian-soldiers expected to respond rapidly to British raids along the Connecticut coast. Three Brockett brothers appeared together on Col. Russell's 1779 endorsement.

narrative_blocks.header: Dropped twenty shillings in fire, Assembly reimbursed him
narrative_blocks.body: Per Connecticut Colonial Records Volume 10, in 1776 Richard Brockett 'dropped a 20-shilling bill in the fire, and on proving that it was accidental, the General Assembly ordered the amount to be made good to him.' The case is one of the few documented Connecticut General Assembly currency-replacement decisions of the Revolutionary era — Continental and state paper currency being fragile, increasingly distrusted, and worth replacing only on solid evidence of innocent loss. The incident captures both the era's currency anxiety and Brockett's reputation: 'his word was as good as his bond.'

narrative_blocks.header: 1779 Alarm List with brothers Ebenezer and Isaiah
narrative_blocks.body: Per Sheldon B. Thorpe's 'North Haven Annals,' in 1779 Colonel Russell issued an order to the officers of the Alarm List and Militia of New Haven — on its back were endorsed the names of Richard Brockett, Ebenezer Brockett, and Isaiah Brockett, three Brockett brothers serving the New Haven militia in the war's middle years. The Alarm List was the call-up roster for civilian-soldiers expected to respond rapidly to British raids along the Connecticut coast. The three-brother endorsement appears in the manuscript record exactly that way.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Brockett brothers married two Pierpont cousins
narrative_blocks.body: Richard Brockett married Mary Pierpont on March 13, 1756 — her grandfather Rev. James Pierpont had founded Yale College and pastored First Church New Haven for thirty years, and her grandmother Mary Hooker Pierpont was the granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker. Richard's brother Abel Brockett married Mary's cousin Hannah Pierpont  — two Brockett brothers married two Pierpont cousins. Mary died at only thirty-four in 1773, but seven children survived: Joseph, Mary, Giles, Lydia, Richard Jr., and two ill-fated infant Jesses.

death.notes: Died on his 63rd birthday — born and died on the same calendar date Jan 17.

marriages.notes: DATE DISCREPANCY: Tree previously had 1782; Brockett Genealogy 1905 (primary family source) gives May 8, 1772. If 1772 correct, Joseph was 15 yrs 3 mos and Rebecca was 15 yrs 5 mos at marriage — extremely young but not impossible in colonial Connecticut. Counter-evidence for 1782: first child Roxanna born Nov 27, 1782 — if married 1772, 10-year gap before first surviving child is unusual but possible. Brockett family book treated as authoritative.

military_service.source_citation: Record of Conn. Men in the Revolution, State Library at Hartford, Manuscript Vol. pp. 547-548
military_service.role: Active Revolutionary War service in Sabin's regiment per the official Connecticut Men in the Revolution record; later rose to Captain in the Connecticut state militia.

narrative_blocks.header: Died on his sixty-third birthday, January 17
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Brockett was born January 17, 1757 in North Haven Connecticut and died January 17, 1820 in North Haven — exactly sixty-three years to the day. The same calendar date marked his entry into and departure from life, a striking biographical coincidence noted in family records and confirmed by his Montowese Cemetery burial. He was buried in the same churchyard where his Congregational parish under Rev. Benjamin Trumbull DD had centered the family's spiritual life.

narrative_blocks.header: Served in Sabin's Connecticut regiment, Revolutionary War
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Brockett's service in the American Revolution is documented in the 'Record of Connecticut Men in the Revolution' at the Connecticut State Library, where his name appears in Lieutenant Colonel Sabin's regiment. After the war he rose to Captain in the Connecticut state militia and was active in town and state affairs. He represented North Haven in the Connecticut General Assembly continuously from 1799 to 1806.

narrative_blocks.header: Through mother Mary Pierpont, Hooker descent confirmed
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Brockett's mother was Mary Pierpont — through whom this branch's Hooker descent runs. She was 'a grand-daughter of Rev. James Pierpont, the successor of Rev. John Davenport as pastor of the First Church in New Haven, and one of the founders of Yale College.' James Pierpont's wife was Mary Hooker, granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker — making Joseph a third-great-grandson of Thomas Hooker on his mother's side.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into Great Barrington Ives family, 1779
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Brockett married James Ives of Great Barrington Massachusetts on June 16, 1779 — at the height of the American Revolution. She was twenty when she married into one of the leading Berkshire County families of western Massachusetts; her husband's town Great Barrington had been founded as the southern boundary of the colonial Berkshire frontier in 1761. Her brother Joseph Brockett would marry Rebecca Tuttle thirteen years before, while Mary's parents Richard and Mary Pierpont Brockett were still alive — Mary's mother died in 1773.

military_service.role: Enlisted as a youth in 1778, served three years through the war's later phase. Listed on the federal Pension List in 1832.

narrative_blocks.header: Three-year teen enlistment, 1832 federal pensioner
narrative_blocks.body: Giles Brockett enlisted at seventeen in 1778 in Colonel Mead's Connecticut Troop and served three full years through the Revolutionary War's later phase. His name appears on the federal Pension List in 1832 — the act that recognized aging Revolutionary veterans. After the war he tried sailing, making one or two voyages to the West Indies, then returned to North Haven to take up farming, where he lived to age eighty-one.

narrative_blocks.header: Sailor first, then deacon and Connecticut legislator
narrative_blocks.body: After his short West Indies sailing career, Giles returned to farming and married Sarah Smith in 1785. In 1803 the family removed to Waterbury Connecticut, where Giles became deacon of the First Congregational Church. He served as deputy to the Connecticut General Court in 1804 and as representative in the Connecticut Legislature in 1809, plus active membership in the Masonic Lodge — a complete local-leadership Yankee farmer profile.

narrative_blocks.header: Six children at East Farms then Dublin Street
narrative_blocks.body: Giles and Sarah's six children included Polly, Sarah, Patty, Harriet (who married Lieutenant Colonel Samuel Peck), Roswell (who died unmarried in Greenville Michigan in 1853), and Lydia (who married Smith Miller). The Waterbury farm was first at East Farms and then on what later became Dublin Street. Giles and Sarah were remembered as 'persons of a happy temperament, lovely, generous, and beloved by their family.'

cross_connections.display_label: Lydia Brockett Blakeslee married Philemon Blakeslee (b.1760). Philemon's sister was.
Lydia Brockett Blakeslee married Philemon Blakeslee (b.1760). Philemon's sister was.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Philemon Blakeslee 1787, nine documented children
narrative_blocks.body: Lydia Brockett married Philemon Blakeslee on February 22, 1787 at age twenty-three. The couple had nine documented children — most without birth dates surviving, but including a daughter who was twin to her son Philemon and died young, and another daughter Lydia who died at age thirty. The most striking generational pattern: daughter Mary Blakeslee later married a cousin Pearly Blakeslee, keeping the Blakeslee surname in two generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Tirzah baptized at St. John's Episcopal, 1792
narrative_blocks.body: Richard Brockett Jr. was born January 9, 1768 and died in 1832 at age sixty-four. His only documented child Tirzah Brockett was born in North Haven in 1792 and baptized that same year at St. John's Episcopal Church — a different church than his parents' Congregational membership at New Haven First Church. The shift to Episcopal practice in Tirzah's baptism marks one of the early-Republic Brockett-family denominational pivots, an era when Connecticut Episcopal congregations were growing from their post-Revolutionary 'Loyalist' status into a recognized mainstream denomination.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived a single day, January 1770
narrative_blocks.body: The first Jesse Brockett was born January 16, 1770 in North Haven and died the very next day — a one-day infant life, the briefest documented in the entire Richard-Mary Brockett seven-child sibling sequence. The name 'Jesse' was attempted a second time two years later when another son was born on February 10, 1772, but that second Jesse lived only thirteen days. Two named-Jesse infant deaths in three years marked the family's mid-1770s grief.

narrative_blocks.header: Second Jesse, died age thirteen days, 1772
narrative_blocks.body: After the first Jesse Brockett died the day after his January 1770 birth, Richard and Mary Pierpont Brockett named their next son Jesse as well — this second Jesse was born February 10, 1772 and lived only thirteen days, dying February 23. The repeated-name pattern was common in colonial families honoring a deceased child's intended name, but the second Jesse's also-early death meant the name passed entirely from this sibling line. The next surviving Jesse Brockett in the family — Jesse 1785-1850, son of Joseph and grandson of Richard — would carry the name forward.

cross_connections.display_label: His paternal aunt — Jonathan Edwards's wife Sarah Pierpont
His Pierpont grandnephew became U.S. Attorney General under Grant

narrative_blocks.header: Wealthiest man in North Haven in 1786
narrative_blocks.body: He was 'accounted to be the wealthiest man in North Haven, Connecticut, in 1786' at age 45, having served as a Sergeant in the Connecticut Militia. He held land that had passed through three generations: from Rev. James Pierpont to son Lt. Joseph Pierpont to him.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife outlived him by nine months
narrative_blocks.body: He died January 16, 1832 at age 90; his wife Elizabeth Cooper Pierpont died November 1, 1832 at age 88. After 66 years of marriage, she survived him by nine months.

narrative_blocks.header: His Pierpont land became 'Dr. Patten place'
narrative_blocks.body: The Pierpont tract eventually passed to his son Capt. Giles Jr., father of Munson Edward Pierpont — described by Thorpe as a distinguished jurist and diplomat. The land later became known locally as 'Dr. Patten place,' marking the end of a four-generation Pierpont landholding.

cross_connections.display_label: The Pierpont land owned by Capt. Giles Pierpont Jr. in.
The Pierpont land owned by Capt. Giles Pierpont Jr. in.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Laura died days after him
narrative_blocks.body: He died December 28, 1842 at age 59 in North Haven. His sixteen-year-old daughter Laura died a few days later, leaving the family doubly bereft in the same week.

narrative_blocks.header: His Pierpont land became Clover Dairy Farm
narrative_blocks.body: The land he inherited had passed from Rev. James Pierpont to Lt. Joseph Pierpont to his father Giles to him — a four-generation Pierpont landholding. After his death it was sold and became Clover Dairy Farm under Dr. D.W. Patten.

narrative_blocks.header: Son became U.S. Attorney General under Grant
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest son was baptized Munson Edwards Pierpont in 1813 — by first wife Eunice Munson, who died eight months later. The son later dropped 'Munson' and changed his surname to Pierrepont, serving as U.S. Attorney General under President Grant in 1875.

narrative_blocks.header: Named her son Talcott to mark the connection
narrative_blocks.body: In 1781 she named her seventh-known child Talcott Ives — using the Talcott family surname as his first name. This naming choice signals that her family was aware of the Talcott-Pierpont-Hooker convergence reaching back through her grandmother's generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters named Miriam — both died young
narrative_blocks.body: Two of her daughters were named Miriam — the first died at age five in 1773, the second born in 1779 lived only briefly. An unnamed infant born in 1771 also died the same year, making three child deaths in this nine-child family.

bio.notes: Given name "Talcott" likely honors the Talcott family — Abigail Pierpont Ives descended through Mary Hooker Pierpont whose daughter-in-law Eunice Talcott (I00054) married Nathaniel Hooker 2d.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved his family from North Haven to Rowe
narrative_blocks.body: He removed his family from North Haven, Connecticut to Rowe in Franklin County, Massachusetts before December 1796 — recorded in North Haven Deeds. His son Isaac Pierpont continued the family there, dying at Rowe in 1866 at age 89.
